confidence.disclosure: Umar II's life is well attested by the Umayyad chroniclers (Tabari, Ibn Sa'd, Ya'qubi) and by later admirers who saw him as the 'fifth rightly-guided caliph'. The major dates are firm: birth around 682 in Medina, governorship of Medina from 706, dismissal in 712, accession to the caliphate at Dabiq in late September 717, and death at Dayr Sim'an in February 720. Several dates are known to the day, including his death (5–10 February 720) and the rough date of his accession (24 September 717). The intermediate movements between Medina, Egypt, Damascus and the various postings are well documented; pilgrimages and hadj appearances during his governorship of Medina are also recorded.

sources: Ibn Abd al-Hakam, Sirat Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz
Julius Wellhausen, The Arab Kingdom and Its Fall (1927)

journey.event: Born around 682 (61–63 AH) in Medina to Abd al-Aziz ibn Marwan and Umm Asim, granddaughter of the second caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab. The family thus links the Umayyad ruling house to the great early caliph by blood.

His father Abd al-Aziz is appointed governor of Egypt by Caliph Marwan I. Young Umar spends much of his childhood in Hulwan, just south of the future site of Cairo, which becomes the seat of his father's governorship.

After Caliph Abd al-Malik recovers Medina from Ibn al-Zubayr, Umar is sent there for his education. Studies with the leading scholars and hadith transmitters of the city; develops the lifelong piety that will mark his caliphate.
His father dies in Egypt. Caliph Abd al-Malik recalls Umar to Damascus and marries him to his own daughter Fatima — a marriage that gives Umar a place at the very heart of the Umayyad ruling house.
Abd al-Malik appoints Umar to the rule of Khanaser, an area near Aleppo in northern Syria. His first administrative posting.

The new caliph al-Walid I, Umar's brother-in-law, appoints him governor of the Hejaz with his seat in Medina. Unlike most Umayyad governors he forms a council of pious scholars to advise him on government, including jurists like Salim ibn Abdullah and Urwah ibn al-Zubayr.
On al-Walid's orders, undertakes the major reconstruction and expansion of the Prophet's Mosque, incorporating the chambers of the Prophet's wives into the enlarged mosque.

Under his lenient rule, the Hejaz becomes a refuge for Iraqi religious and political dissidents fleeing the brutal eastern viceroy al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf. So many flee to Medina that al-Hajjaj begins pressing al-Walid to remove Umar.
Dismissed as governor in May or June 712 at al-Hajjaj's insistence. Returns to the caliph's court in Damascus but remains in al-Walid's favour as the brother of his first wife.
Caliph al-Walid dies. Umar leads the funeral prayers for him. Becomes a leading advisor to the new caliph Sulayman, alongside the religious scholar Raja ibn Haywa.
Travels with Caliph Sulayman on the hajj pilgrimage to Mecca and then to Jerusalem, serving as one of his closest counsellors.

On 24 September 717 (10 Safar 99 AH), Caliph Sulayman dies at the military camp at Dabiq in northern Syria, where he is preparing the great siege of Constantinople. On his deathbed — at Raja ibn Haywa's urging — he names his cousin Umar as successor, bypassing his own brothers and sons. Umar weeps when he hears the news.

Refuses the imperial regalia offered to him in Damascus, choosing his own mule over the state carriage. Returns the lavish Umayyad family estates and palaces to the public treasury. Dismisses unpopular governors and reforms the system of taxation so that converts to Islam are no longer subject to the discriminatory jizya.

Orders the immediate withdrawal of the Muslim army from the failed second siege of Constantinople. The Arab forces retreat in disarray during the bitter winter; tens of thousands die. Umar's calculation is that the Caliphate cannot afford the bleeding.

Restructures the eastern provinces, breaking up the vast governorship that al-Hajjaj had built. Imprisons the unjust governor Yazid ibn al-Muhallab. Sends missionaries to the Berbers of North Africa and the Turks of Central Asia, the only Umayyad caliph to do so. Returns the estate of Fadak to the descendants of Fatima, the Prophet's daughter.

Falls ill on the road back from Damascus and dies between 5 and 10 February 720 (in Rajab 101 AH) at the village of Dayr Sim'an, near Maarat al-Numan in northern Syria. His caliphate has lasted only two years and five months. He is buried on a small plot of land he had bought with his own money, and his tomb still stands. Some sources say he was poisoned by Umayyad relatives who feared he would name his own son successor; others record only that he died exhausted by his reforms, aged 39.
